retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Technique or Treatment -- Gingival Flap Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Gingival flap procedures are typically employed when there is residual pocketing without an underlying bone defect, except for generalized bone loss as in suprabony pockets. Alternative names for gingival flap procedures are "open flap debridement" or "surgical scaling and root planing." Widman was one of the first surgeons to describe a gingival flap for pocket reduction. [27] (see Box 2)

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Technique or Treatment -- Gingival Flap Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: The modified Widman flap is frequently used as a control treatment in clinical trials of periodontal surgery techniques. It differs from a conventional gingival flap in that incisions are kept close to the root surface, and tissue is only reflected enough to allow access to the root surface. Conceptually, the opposite of a modified Widman flap is the apically positioned flap, which features much flap reflection and suturing of the flap margins apical to the alveolar crest by suturing it to the vestibular periosteum. The apically positioned flap may be used with osseous surgery to achieve minimal interdental thickness and maximized probing depth reduction. Unlike the modified Widman flap, the apically positioned flap is associated with significant generalized gingival recession.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Anatomical Considerations for Flap Design
retrieved_docs.content: Flaps in the maxilla must avoid damaging the greater palatine neurovascular bundle and the incisive papilla. The palate is typically used for connective tissue graft harvesting. The greater palatine bundle runs on the palate vault from posterior to anterior at a distance close to the alveolar ridge. [17] Injury to the greater palatine bundle is associated with prolonged bleeding and healing complications. The incisive papilla between the maxillary incisors contains soft tissue and a vestigial nerve in adults. It can be cut and elevated if needed, allowing the incisive canal's contents to be removed for grafting.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Flap Design -- Technique or Treatment -- Flaps for Surgical Exposure
retrieved_docs.content: Envelope flap: The envelope flap is a widely used design that involves a horizontal incision along the gingival margin, including the interdental papillae. [10] This flap is indicated for minor surgical procedures, such as tooth extractions and small bone grafts. This flap is particularly advantageous in palate surgery due to the concavity of the bone (see Image. Envelope Flap). [2] The technical considerations and risks associated with this flap are listed below.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Indications -- Lateral Sliding / Pedicle Flap
retrieved_docs.content: Correction of single localized gingival recession or mucogingival defect with a normal amount of keratinized gingiva adjacent to the defect

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Technique or Treatment -- Lateral Sliding/Pedicle Flap
retrieved_docs.content: Small, localized defects of keratinized gingiva adjacent to a broader area of gingiva can be treated with a lateral sliding pedicle flap, where adjacent tissue is mobilized with a split-thickness incision and advanced over the defect site onto denuded gingiva. [70] In cases of narrowly attached gingiva, a modified apically positioned flap can be employed to split the attached gingiva and apically position the flap's apical side, thus gaining gingiva through secondary intention healing. [71] (see Box 6.2)

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Technique or Treatment -- Gingival Flap Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: Personnel may opt for other incision designs, instruments, and suturing techniques based on treatment goals, personal preference, and local anatomy. Variants of the gingival flap procedure differ by the number of incisions, locations of the incisions, and extent of the reflection of the flap. A notable variant is the modified Widman flap, which uses sulcular, submarginal, and connecting incisions for minimal tissue reflection and removal, resulting in minimal gingival recession. The improvement in attachment and probing depth is most likely due to the formation of a junctional epithelium. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Axial Flaps -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the indications for use of axial flaps. Describe the anatomy and physiology of axial flaps. Summarize the most common complications associated with axial flaps. Explain the importance of post-operative monitoring for axial flaps. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Flap Design -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The interdental papillae should be spared, and incisions over bony prominences should be avoided to ensure a tension-free flap closure, thereby reducing the risks of necrosis and dehiscence. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Technique or Treatment -- Connective Tissue Grafts and Acellular Dermal Matrix Grafts
retrieved_docs.content: While connective tissue grafting or acellular dermal matrix grafts are typically used for treating localized recession defects, other procedures can also treat localized recession defects. Single localized gingival recession adjacent to a wide keratinized gingiva can be treated with a lateral sliding flap or a double pedicle flap. These techniques avoid donor site surgery and provide excellent color match while increasing the amount of keratinized tissue. The main limitation of these procedures is that case selection and surgical technique are critical to the success of these procedures. The coronally advanced flap is another method for resolving moderate or shallow localized recession defects on multiple teeth. However, the coronally advanced flap by itself is less effective than the coronally advanced flap combined with a connective tissue graft or collagen matrix. [77] The coronally advanced flap is typically used as a control procedure in clinical trials evaluating various surgery techniques and materials for treating gingival recession defects. [78]

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Indications -- Free/Autogenous Gingival Grafts
retrieved_docs.content: Correction of mucogingival defects or gain in keratinized gingiva Localized deepening of the vestibule

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Complications -- Crown Lengthening Surgeries
retrieved_docs.content: Teeth that have undergone crown lengthening surgeries will appear longer than before, and the interdental papilla will no longer reach the original interproximal contact. Therefore, restorations must account for these changes by having longer interproximal contacts. Teeth undergoing crown lengthening surgery must also be provisionalized for at least 6 weeks after crown lengthening. Patients must be instructed on how to perform oral hygiene for provisional restorations and prevent accidental fracture or dislodgment of provisional restorations. Since crown lengthening removes crestal bone and available bone height to vital structures, in rare cases, crown lengthening may preclude future implant therapy as there may no longer be enough bone for the placement of implants. In the anterior maxilla, crown lengthening of teeth on one side may require matching crown lengthening on the other side to achieve an esthetic result.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Free gingival and autogenous connective tissue grafting may be challenging if the palatal vault is shallow, contains a palatal torus, or features thin palatal tissue. Pedicle flaps are challenging to perform for broad gingival defects and are unlikely to succeed if adjacent gingival tissues are limited.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Flap Design -- Technique or Treatment -- Flaps for Reconstruction
retrieved_docs.content: Technique A Doppler probe is used to identify the buccal artery, and the parotid duct is cannulated to prevent damage to these structures. The flap is outlined and incised, with the anterior extent positioned just posterior to the oral commissure if that length is necessary, and the upper extent placed just below the parotid duct. The inferior incision may be as low as necessary, but care should be taken to avoid effacing the gingivobuccal sulcus if the flap is too broad. The flap is elevated from the underlying buccopharyngeal fascia and may include fat, as needed. Elevation proceeds from anterior to posterior, ensuring the buccal artery and venous plexus of the buccinator are preserved. If the exposed pedicle could pose a problem due to its course, the posterior mucosa and muscle may be divided, creating a narrower pedicle that can be tunneled instead of crossing over the teeth. Once the flap is transposed into the defect, the donor site can be closed primarily and allowed to heal by secondary intention, or a combination of both approaches may be used. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The 2 basic surgical flap designs used in dentoalveolar surgery are full-thickness mucoperiosteal flap and split-thickness mucoperiosteal flap. [32] Maintenance of flap perfusion is critical. The tissue flap should have a broad base to ensure proper blood supply to the flap margins.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1619
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is described as a broad surgical approach that encompasses a variety of operative techniques used to remove tissue, explore anatomical regions, or treat disease. Specific procedures classified under the Excision method include partial or complete adrenalectomy with or without adrenal gland biopsy performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal routes; cholecystectomy combined with exploration of the common bile duct, with or without choledochoenterostomy; exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of both infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial locations; suboccipital craniectomy aimed at cranial‑nerve exploration or decompression; radical orchiectomy for tumor removal that incorporates abdominal exploration; and parathyroidectomy procedures that involve mediastinal or cervical exploration, sometimes requiring a sternal split or transthoracic approach. In addition, Excision is linked to other therapeutic interventions on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, indicating that it

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 884
retrieved_docs.content: is identified as a possibly included method of exploration. Cranial exploration procedures include exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region, supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy, and suboccipital craniectomy performed for exploration or decompression of cranial nerves. An orbit can be explored via a transcranial approach with lesion removal, which is likewise categorized as an exploration method. Spinal exploration is represented by laminectomy procedures that combine exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without additional facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy. These laminectomies are specified for one or two lumbar vertebral segments (e.g., for spinal stenosis) and are noted as possibly included methods. Additional laminectomy variants cover more extensive cervical or lumbar segments (more than two vertebral levels) and are also listed as possibly included methods of exploration. Excision is also the method for “other or therapeutic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx,” and it is linked to plastic procedures on the nose, indicating a relationship between excisional techniques and nasal aesthetic surgery. Within the hierarchical structure of clinical actions, the group labeled “Clinical actions A‑G” is a child of Excision, making Excision the parent concept for these actions. Conversely, certain spinal operations are explicitly excluded from the Excision category: laminectomy with exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina (without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy) at lumbar, sacral, or thoracic levels (one or two vertebral segments) are

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1502
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is classified as a clinical action within the broader group “Clinical actions A‑G” and serves as an umbrella term for a wide range of operative procedures. It includes adrenalectomy—whether partial or complete, performed transabdominally, laparoscopically, or dorsally, with or without biopsy and with possible removal of adjacent retroperitoneal tumor—as well as several biliary surgeries such as cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, and associated cholangiography, which can be performed via open or laparoscopic techniques. Craniectomy or craniotomy performed exploratively in the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region also falls under Excision, as do various parathyroidectomy approaches—including cervical, sternal‑split, and transthoracic routes—each potentially combined with mediastinal exploration. In spinal surgery, Excision encompasses laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and excision of herniated intervertebral disc at a single lumbar level, but explicitly excludes laminectomy procedures that involve only exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for spinal stenosis across cervical and thoracic segments (both 1‑2 vertebral levels and more extensive segments). Additionally, Excision covers “other or therapeutic procedures on nose, mouth and pharynx” and is linked to plastic procedures on the nose, highlighting its relevance to both general surgical and specialized reconstructive interventions. This comprehensive definition clarifies the scope of Excision, distinguishing it from specific laminectomy techniques while integrating diverse surgical modalities under a single procedural category.
